html:
V

Adam woke up feeling terribly ill. He rang his bell once or twice, but nobody came. Later he woke up again and rang the bell. The Italian waiter appeared, undulating slightly in the doorway. Adam ordered breakfast. Lottie came in and sat on his bed.

вАЬHad a nice breakfast, dear?вАЭ she said.

вАЬNot yet,вАЭ said Adam. вАЬIвАЩve only just woken up.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs right,вАЭ said Lottie. вАЬNothing like a nice breakfast. There was a young lady for you on the phone, but I canвАЩt remember what it was she said at the minute. WeвАЩve all been upside down this morning. Such a fuss. Had the police in, we have, ever since I donвАЩt know what time, drinking up my wine and asking questions and putting their noses where theyвАЩre not wanted. All because Flossie must needs go and swing on the chandelier. She never had any sense, Flossie. Well, sheвАЩs learned her lesson now, poor girl. Whoever heard of such a thingвБ†вАФswinging on a chandelier. Poor Judge WhatвАЩs-his-name is in a terrible state about it. I said to him itвАЩs not so much the price of the chandelier, I said. What money can make, money can mend, I said, and thatвАЩs the truth, isnвАЩt it, dear? But what I mind, I said, is having a death in the house and all the fuss. It doesnвАЩt do anyone any good having people killing theirselves in a house like Flossie did. Now what may you want, my Italian queen?вАЭ said Lottie as the waiter came in with a tray, the smell of kippers contending with nuit de Noel rather disagreeably.

вАЬGentlemanвАЩs breakfast,вАЭ said the waiter.

вАЬAnd how many more breakfasts do you think he wants, I should like to know? HeвАЩs had his breakfast hours ago while you were powdering your nose downstairs, havenвАЩt you, dear?вАЭ

вАЬNo,вАЭ said Adam, вАЬas a matter of fact, no.вАЭ

вАЬThere, do you hear what the gentleman says? He doesnвАЩt want two breakfasts. DonвАЩt stand there wiggling your behind at me. Take it away quick or IвАЩll catch you such a smack.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ ThatвАЩs just the wayвБ†вАФonce you get the police in everyone gets all upset. ThereвАЩs that boy brings you two breakfasts and I dare say thereвАЩs some poor fellow along the passage somewhere who hasnвАЩt had any breakfast at all. You canвАЩt get anywhere without a nice breakfast. Half the young fellows as come here now donвАЩt have anything except a cachet Faivre and some orange juice. ItвАЩs not right,вАЭ said Lottie, вАЬand IвАЩve spoken to that boy about using scent twenty times if IвАЩve spoken once.вАЭ

The waiterвАЩs head appeared, and with it another wave of nuit de Noel.

вАЬIf you please, madam, the inspectors want to speak to you downstairs, madam.вАЭ

вАЬAll right, my little bird of paradise, IвАЩll be there.вАЭ

Lottie trotted away and the waiter came sidling back bearing his tray of kippers and leering at Adam with a horrible intimacy.

вАЬTurn on my bath, will you, please,вАЭ said Adam.

вАЬAlas, there is a gentleman asleep in the bath. Shall I wake him?вАЭ

вАЬNo, it doesnвАЩt matter.вАЭ

вАЬWill that be all, sir?вАЭ

вАЬYes, thank you.вАЭ

The waiter stood about fingering the brass knobs at the end of the bed, smiling ingratiatingly. Then he produced from under his coat a gardenia, slightly browned at the edges. (He had found it in an evening coat he had just been brushing.)

Would the signor perhaps like a buttonhole?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Madame Crump was so severeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ it was nice sometimes to be able to have a talk with the gentlemenвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬNo,вАЭ said Adam. вАЬGo away.вАЭ For he had a headache.

The waiter sighed deeply, and walked with pettish steps to the door; sighed again and took the gardenia to the gentleman in the bathroom.

Adam ate some breakfast. No kipper, he reflected, is ever as good as it smells; how this too earthly contact with flesh and bone spoiled the first happy exhilaration; if only one could live, as Jehovah was said to have done, on the savour of burnt offerings. He lay back for a little in his bed thinking about the smells of food, of the greasy horror of fried fish and the deeply moving smell that came from it; of the intoxicating breath of bakeries and the dullness of buns.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He planned dinners of enchanting aromatic foods that should be carried under the nose, snuffed and thrown to the dogsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ endless dinners, in which one could alternate flavour with flavour from sunset to dawn without satiety, while one breathed great draughts of the bouquet of old brandy.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Oh for the wings of a dove, thought Adam, wandering a little from the point as he fell asleep again (everyone is liable to this ninetyish feeling in the early morning after a party).

Presently the telephone by AdamвАЩs bed began ringing.

вАЬHullo, yes.вАЭ

вАЬLady to speak to you.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Hullo, is that you, Adam?вАЭ

вАЬIs that Nina?вАЭ

вАЬHow are you, my darling?вАЭ

вАЬOh, Nina.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬMy poor sweet, I feel like that, too. Listen, angel. You havenвАЩt forgotten that youвАЩre going to see my papa today, have youвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ or have you? IвАЩve just sent him a wire to say that youвАЩre going to lunch with him. DвАЩyou know where he lives?вАЭ

вАЬBut youвАЩre coming too?вАЭ

вАЬWell, no. I donвАЩt think I will, if you donвАЩt mind.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ IвАЩve got rather a pain.вАЭ

вАЬMy dear, if you knew what a pain IвАЩve got.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬYes, but thatвАЩs different, darling. Anyway, thereвАЩs no object in our both going.вАЭ

вАЬBut what am I to say?вАЭ

вАЬDarling, donвАЩt be tiresome. You know perfectly well. Just ask him for some money.вАЭ

вАЬWill he like that?вАЭ

вАЬYes, darling, of course he will. Why will you go on? IвАЩve got to get up now. Goodbye. Take care of yourself.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Ring me up when you get back and tell me what papa said. By the way, have you seen the paper this morning?вБ†вАФthereвАЩs something so funny about last night. Too bad of Van. Goodbye.вАЭ

While Adam was dressing, he realized that he did not know where he was to go. He rang up again. вАЬBy the way, Nina, where does your papa live?вАЭ

вАЬDidnвАЩt I tell you? ItвАЩs a house called Doubting, and itвАЩs all falling down really. You go to Aylesbury by train and then take a taxi. TheyвАЩre the most expensive taxis in the world, too.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Have you got any money?вАЭ

Adam looked on the dressing-table: вАЬAbout seven shillings,вАЭ he said.

вАЬMy dear, thatвАЩs not enough. YouвАЩll have to make poor papa pay for the taxi.вАЭ

вАЬWill he like that?вАЭ

вАЬYes, of course, heвАЩs an angel.вАЭ

вАЬI wish youвАЩd come too, Nina.вАЭ

вАЬDarling, I told you. IвАЩve got such a pain.вАЭ

Downstairs, as Lottie had said, everything was upside down. That is to say that there were policemen and reporters teeming in every corner of the hotel, each with a bottle of champagne and a glass. Lottie, Doge, Judge Skimp, the Inspector, four plain-clothes men and the body were in Judge SkimpвАЩs suite.

вАЬWhat is not clear to me, sir,вАЭ said the Inspector, вАЬis what prompted the young lady to swing on the chandelier. Not wishing to cause offence, sir, and begging your pardon, was sheвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАК?вАЭ

вАЬYes,вАЭ said Judge Skimp, вАЬshe was.вАЭ

вАЬExactly,вАЭ said the Inspector. вАЬA clear case of misadventure, eh, Mrs.¬†Crump? ThereвАЩll have to be an inquest, of course, but I think probably I shall be able to arrange things so that there is no mention of your name in the case, sirвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ well, thatвАЩs very kind of you, Mrs.¬†Crump, perhaps just one more glass.вАЭ

вАЬLottie,вАЭ said Adam, вАЬcan you lend me some money?вАЭ

вАЬMoney, dear? Of course. Doge, have you got any money?вАЭ

вАЬI was asleep at the time myself, mum, and was not even made aware of the occurrence until I was called this morning. Being slightly deaf, the sound of the disasterвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬJudge WhatвАЩs-your-name, got any money?вАЭ

вАЬI should take it as a great privilege if I could be of any assistanceвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs right, give some to young Thingummy here. That all you want, deary? DonвАЩt run away. WeвАЩre just thinking of having a little drink.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ No, not that wine, dear, itвАЩs what we keep for the police. IвАЩve just ordered a better bottle if my young butterfly would bring it along.вАЭ

Adam had a glass of champagne, hoping it would make him feel a little better. It made him feel much worse.

Then he went to Marylebone. It was Armistice Day, and they were selling artificial poppies in the streets. As he reached the station it struck eleven and for two minutes all over the country everyone was quiet and serious. Then he went to Aylesbury, reading on the way BalcairnвАЩs account of Archie SchwertвАЩs party. He was pleased to see himself described as вАЬthe brilliant young novelist,вАЭ and wondered whether NinaвАЩs papa read gossip paragraphs, and supposed not. The two women opposite him in the carriage obviously did.

вАЬI no sooner opened the paper,вАЭ said one, вАЬthan I was on the phone at once to all the ladies of the committee, and weвАЩd sent off a wire to our Member before one oвАЩclock. We know how to make things hum at the Bois. IвАЩve got a copy of what we sent. Look. Members of the Committee of the LadiesвАЩ Conservative Association at Chesham Bois wish to express their extreme displeasure at reports in this morningвАЩs paper of midnight party at No.¬†10. They call upon Captain CrutwellвБ†вАФthatвАЩs our Member; such a nice stamp of manвБ†вАФstrenuously to withhold support to Prime Minister. It cost nearly four shillings, but, as I said at the time, it was not a moment to spoil the ship for a haвАЩpвАЩorth of tar. DonвАЩt you agree, Mrs.¬†Ithewaite?вАЭ

вАЬI do, indeed, Mrs.¬†Orraway-Smith. It is clearly a case in which a mandate from the constituencies is required. IвАЩll talk to our chairwoman at Wendover.вАЭ

вАЬYes, do, Mrs.¬†Ithewaite. It is in a case like this that the womanвАЩs vote can count.вАЭ

вАЬIf itвАЩs a choice between my moral judgement and the nationalization of banking, I prefer nationalization, if you see what I mean.вАЭ

вАЬExactly what I think. Such a terrible example to the lower classes, apart from everything.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs what I mean. ThereвАЩs our Agnes, now. How can I stop her having young men in the kitchen when she knows that Sir James Brown has parties like that at all hours of the night.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

They were both wearing hats like nothing on earth, which bobbed and nodded as they spoke.

At Aylesbury Adam got into a Ford taxi and asked to be taken to a house called Doubting.

вАЬDoubting вАЩAll?вАЭ

вАЬWell, I suppose so. Is it falling down?вАЭ

вАЬCould do with a lick of paint,вАЭ said the driver, a spotty youth. вАЬName of Blount.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs it.вАЭ

вАЬLong way from here Doubting вАЩAll is. Cost you fifteen bob.вАЭ

вАЬAll right.вАЭ

вАЬIf youвАЩre a commercial, I can tell you straight it ainвАЩt no use going to вАЩim. Young feller asked me the way there this morning. Driving a Morris. Wanted to sell him a vacuum cleaner. Old boy вАЩad answered an advertisement asking for a demonstration. When he got there the old boy wouldnвАЩt even look at it. Can you beat that?вАЭ

вАЬNo, IвАЩm not trying to sell him anythingвБ†вАФat least not exactly.вАЭ

вАЬPersonal visit, perhaps.вАЭ

вАЬYes.вАЭ

вАЬAh.вАЭ

Satisfied that his passenger was in earnest about the journey, the taxi-driver put on some coatsвБ†вАФfor it was rainingвБ†вАФgot out of his seat and cranked up the engine. Presently they started.

They drove for a mile or two past bungalows and villas and timbered public houses to a village in which every house seemed to be a garage and filling station. Here they left the main road and AdamвАЩs discomfort became acute.

At last they came to twin octagonal lodges and some heraldic gateposts and large wrought-iron gates, behind which could be seen a broad sweep of ill-kept drive.

вАЬDoubting вАЩAll,вАЭ said the driver.

He blew his horn once or twice, but no lodge-keeperвАЩs wife, aproned and apple-cheeked, appeared to bob them in. He got out and shook the gates reproachfully.

вАЬChained-and-locked,вАЭ he said. вАЬTry another way.вАЭ

They drove on for another mile; on the side of the Hall the road was bordered by dripping trees and a dilapidated stone wall; presently they reached some cottages and a white gate. This they opened and turned into a rough track, separated from the park by low iron railings. There were sheep grazing on either side. One of them had strayed into the drive. It fled before them in a frenzied trot, stopping and looking round over its dirty tail and then plunging on again until its agitation brought it to the side of the path, where they overtook it and passed it.

The track led to some stables, then behind rows of hothouses, among potting-sheds and heaps of drenched leaves, past nondescript outbuildings that had once been laundry and bakery and brewhouse and a huge kennel where once someone had kept a bear, until suddenly it turned by a clump of holly and elms and laurel bushes into an open space that had once been laid with gravel. A lofty Palladian fa√Іade stretched before them and in front of it an equestrian statue pointed a baton imperiously down the main drive.

вАЬвАКвАЩEre yвАЩare,вАЭ said the driver.

Adam paid him and went up the steps to the front door. He rang the bell and waited. Nothing happened. Presently he rang again. At this moment the door opened.

вАЬDonвАЩt ring twice,вАЭ said a very angry old man. вАЬWhat do you want?вАЭ

вАЬIs Mr.¬†Blount in?вАЭ

вАЬThereвАЩs no Mr.¬†Blount here. This is Colonel BlountвАЩs house.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm sorry.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I think the Colonel is expecting me to luncheon.вАЭ

вАЬNonsense. IвАЩm Colonel Blount,вАЭ and he shut the door.

The Ford had disappeared. It was still raining hard. Adam rang again.

вАЬYes,вАЭ said Colonel Blount, appearing instantly.

вАЬI wonder if youвАЩd let me telephone to the station for a taxi?вАЭ

вАЬNot on the telephone.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ ItвАЩs raining. Why donвАЩt you come in? ItвАЩs absurd to walk to the station in this. Have you come about the vacuum cleaner?вАЭ

вАЬNo.вАЭ

вАЬFunny, IвАЩve been expecting a man all the morning to show me a vacuum cleaner. Come in, do. WonвАЩt you stay to luncheon?вАЭ

вАЬI should love to.вАЭ

вАЬSplendid. I get very little company nowadays. You must forgive me for opening the door to you myself. My butler is in bed today. He suffers terribly in his feet when it is wet. Both my footmen were killed in the war.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Put your hat and coat here. I hope you havenвАЩt got wet.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ IвАЩm sorry you didnвАЩt bring the vacuum cleanerвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ but never mind. How are you?вАЭ he said, suddenly holding out his hand.

They shook hands and Colonel Blount led the way down a long corridor, lined with marble busts on yellow marble pedestals, to a large room full of furniture, with a fire burning in a fine rococo fireplace. There was a large leather-topped walnut writing-table under a window opening on to a terrace. Colonel Blount picked up a telegram and read it.

вАЬIвАЩd quite forgotten,вАЭ he said in some confusion. вАЬIвАЩm afraid youвАЩll think me very discourteous, but it is, after all, impossible for me to ask you to luncheon. I have a guest coming on very intimate family business. You understand, donвАЩt you?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ To tell you the truth, itвАЩs some young rascal who wants to marry my daughter. I must see him alone to discuss settlements.вАЭ

вАЬWell, I want to marry your daughter, too,вАЭ said Adam.

вАЬWhat an extraordinary coincidence. Are you sure you do?вАЭ

вАЬPerhaps the telegram may be about me. What does it say?вАЭ

вАЬвАКвАШEngaged to marry Adam Symes. Expect him luncheon. Nina.вАЩ Are you Adam Symes?вАЭ

вАЬYes.вАЭ

вАЬMy dear boy, why didnвАЩt you say so before, instead of going on about a vacuum cleaner? How are you?вАЭ

They shook hands again.

вАЬIf you donвАЩt mind,вАЭ said Colonel Blount, вАЬwe will keep our business until after luncheon. IвАЩm afraid everything is looking very bare at present. You must come down and see the gardens in the summer. We had some lovely hydrangeas last year. I donвАЩt think I shall live here another winter. Too big for an old man. I was looking at some of the houses theyвАЩre putting up outside Aylesbury. Did you see them coming along? Nice little red houses. Bathroom and everything. Quite cheap, too, and near the cinematographs. I hope you are fond of the cinematograph too? The Rector and I go a great deal. I hope youвАЩll like the Rector. Common little man rather. But heвАЩs got a motor car, useful that. How long are you staying?вАЭ

вАЬI promised Nina IвАЩd be back tonight.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs a pity. They change the film at the Electra Palace. We might have gone.вАЭ

An elderly woman servant came in to announce luncheon. вАЬWhat is at the Electra Palace, do you know, Mrs.¬†Florin?вАЭ

вАЬGreta Garbo in Venetian Kisses, I think, sir.вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt really think I like Greta Garbo. IвАЩve tried to,вАЭ said Colonel Blount, вАЬbut I just donвАЩt.вАЭ

They went in to luncheon in a huge dining-room dark with family portraits.

вАЬIf you donвАЩt mind,вАЭ said Colonel Blount, вАЬI prefer not to talk at meals.вАЭ

He propped a morocco-bound volume of Punch before his plate against a vast silver urn, from which grew a small castor-oil plant.

вАЬGive Mr.¬†Symes a book,вАЭ he said.

Mrs.¬†Florin put another volume of Punch beside Adam.

вАЬIf you come across anything really funny read it to me,вАЭ said Colonel Blount.

Then they had luncheon.

They were nearly an hour over luncheon. Course followed course in disconcerting abundance while Colonel Blount ate and ate, turning the leaves of his book and chuckling frequently. They ate hare soup and boiled turbot and stewed sweetbreads and black Bredenham ham with Madeira sauce and roast pheasant and a rum omelette and toasted cheese and fruit. First they drank sherry, then claret, then port. Then Colonel Blount shut his book with a broad sweep of his arm rather as the headmaster of AdamвАЩs private school used to shut the Bible after evening prayers, folded his napkin carefully and stuffed it into a massive silver ring, muttered some words of grace and finally stood up, saying:

вАЬWell, I donвАЩt know about you, but IвАЩm going to have a little nap,вАЭ and trotted out of the room.

вАЬThereвАЩs a fire in the library, sir,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Florin. вАЬIвАЩll bring you your coffee there. The Colonel doesnвАЩt have coffee, he finds it interferes with his afternoon sleep. What time would you like your afternoon tea, sir?вАЭ

вАЬI ought really to be getting back to London. How long will it be before the Colonel comes down, do you think?вАЭ

вАЬWell, it all depends, sir. Not usually till about five or half-past. Then he reads until dinner at seven and after dinner gets the Rector to drive him in to the pictures. A sedentary life, as you might say.вАЭ

She led Adam into the library and put a silver coffeepot at his elbow.

вАЬIвАЩll bring you tea at four,вАЭ she said.

Adam sat in front of the fire in a deep armchair. Outside the rain beat on the double windows. There were several magazines in the libraryвБ†вАФmostly cheap weeklies devoted to the cinema. There was a stuffed owl and a case of early British remains, bone pins and bits of pottery and a skull, which had been dug up in the park many years ago and catalogued by NinaвАЩs governess. There was a cabinet containing the relics of NinaвАЩs various collecting feversвБ†вАФsome butterflies and a beetle or two, some fossils and some birdsвАЩ eggs and a few postage stamps. There were some bookcases of superbly unreadable books, a gun, a butterfly net, an alpenstock in the corner. There were catalogues of agricultural machines and acetylene plants, lawn mowers, вАЬsports requisites.вАЭ There was a fire screen worked with a coat of arms. The chimneypiece was hung with the embroidered saddle-cloths of Colonel BlountвАЩs regiment of Lancers. There was an engraving of all the members of the Royal Yacht Squadron, with a little plan in the corner, marked to show who was who. There were many other things of equal interest besides, but before Adam had noticed any more he was fast asleep.

Mrs.¬†Florin woke him at four. The coffee had disappeared and its place was taken by a silver tray with a lace cloth on it. There was a silver teapot, and a silver kettle with a little spirit-lamp underneath, and a silver cream jug and a covered silver dish full of muffins. There was also hot buttered toast and honey and gentlemanвАЩs relish and a chocolate cake, a cherry cake, a seed cake and a fruit cake and some tomato sandwiches and pepper and salt and currant bread and butter.

вАЬWould you care for a lightly boiled egg, sir? The Colonel generally has one if heвАЩs awake.вАЭ

вАЬNo, thank you,вАЭ said Adam. He felt a thousand times better for his rest. When Nina and he were married, he thought, they would often come down there for the day after a really serious party. For the first time he noticed an obese liver and white spaniel, which was waking up, too, on the hearthrug.

вАЬPlease not to give her muffins,вАЭ said Mrs.¬†Florin, вАЬitвАЩs the one thing sheвАЩs not supposed to have, and the Colonel will give them to her. He loves that dog,вАЭ she added with a burst of confidence. вАЬTakes her to the pictures with him of an evening. Not that she can appreciate them really like a human can.вАЭ

Adam gave herвБ†вАФthe spaniel, not Mrs.¬†FlorinвБ†вАФa gentle prod with his foot and a lump of sugar. She licked his shoe with evident cordiality. Adam was not above feeling flattered by friendliness in dogs.

He had finished his tea and was filling his pipe when Colonel Blount came into the library.

вАЬWho the devil are you?вАЭ said his host.

вАЬAdam Symes,вАЭ said Adam.

вАЬNever heard of you. How did you get in? Who gave you tea? What do you want?вАЭ

вАЬYou asked me to luncheon,вАЭ said Adam. вАЬI came about being married to Nina.вАЭ

вАЬMy dear boy, of course. How absurd of me. IвАЩve such a bad memory for names. It comes of seeing so few people. How are you?вАЭ

They shook hands again.

вАЬSo youвАЩre the young man whoвАЩs engaged to Nina,вАЭ said the Colonel, eyeing him for the first time in the way prospective sons-in-laws are supposed to be eyed. вАЬNow what in the world do you want to get married for? I shouldnвАЩt, you know, really I shouldnвАЩt. Are you rich?вАЭ

вАЬNo, not at present, IвАЩm afraid, thatвАЩs rather what I wanted to talk about.вАЭ

вАЬHow much money have you got?вАЭ

вАЬWell, sir, actually at the moment I havenвАЩt got any at all.вАЭ

вАЬWhen did you last have any?вАЭ

вАЬI had a thousand pounds last night, but I gave it all to a drunk Major.вАЭ

вАЬWhy did you do that?вАЭ

вАЬWell, I hoped heвАЩd put it on Indian Runner for the November Handicap.вАЭ

вАЬNever heard of the horse. DidnвАЩt he?вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt think he can have.вАЭ

вАЬWhen will you next have some money?вАЭ

вАЬWhen IвАЩve written some books.вАЭ

вАЬHow many books?вАЭ

вАЬTwelve.вАЭ

вАЬHow much will you have then?вАЭ

вАЬProbably fifty pounds advance on my thirteenth book.вАЭ

вАЬAnd how long will it take you to write twelve books?вАЭ

вАЬAbout a year.вАЭ

вАЬHow long would it take most people?вАЭ

вАЬAbout twenty years. Of course, put like that I do see that it sounds rather hopelessвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ but, you see, Nina and I hoped that you, that is, that perhaps for the next year until I get my twelve books written, that you might help usвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬHow could I help you? IвАЩve never written a book in my life.вАЭ

вАЬNo, we thought you might give us some money.вАЭ

вАЬYou thought that, did you?вАЭ

вАЬYes, thatвАЩs what we thoughtвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Colonel Blount looked at him gravely for some time. Then he said, вАЬI think that an admirable idea. I donвАЩt see any reason at all why I shouldnвАЩt. How much do you want?вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs really terribly good of you, sirвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Well, you know, just enough to live on quietly for a bit. I hardly knowвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬWell, would a thousand pounds be any help?вАЭ

вАЬYes, it would indeed. We shall both be terribly grateful.вАЭ

вАЬNot at all, my dear boy. Not at all. What did you say your name was?вАЭ

вАЬAdam Symes.вАЭ

Colonel Blount went to the table and wrote out a cheque. вАЬThere you are,вАЭ he said. вАЬNow donвАЩt go giving that away to another drunk major.вАЭ

вАЬReally, sir! I donвАЩt know how to thank you. NinaвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬNot another word. Now I expect that you will want to be off to London again. WeвАЩll send Mrs.¬†Florin across to the Rectory and make the Rector drive you to the station. Useful having a neighbour with a motor car. They charge fivepence on the buses from here to Aylesbury. Robbers.вАЭ

It does not befall many young men to be given a thousand pounds by a complete stranger twice on successive evenings. Adam laughed aloud in the RectorвАЩs car as they drove to the station. The Rector, who had been in the middle of writing a sermon and resented with daily increasing feeling Colonel BlountвАЩs neighbourly appropriation of his car and himself, kept his eyes fixed on the streaming windscreen, pretending not to notice. Adam laughed all the way to Aylesbury, sitting and holding his knees and shaking all over. The Rector could hardly bring himself to say good night when they parted in the station yard.

There was half an hour to wait for a train and the leaking roof and wet railway lines had a sobering effect on Adam. He bought an evening paper. On the front page was an exquisitely funny photograph of Miss Runcible in Hawaiian costume tumbling down the steps of No.¬†10 Downing Street. The Government had fallen that afternoon, he read, being defeated on a motion rising from the answer to a question about the treatment of Miss Runcible by Customs House officers. It was generally held in Parliamentary circles that the deciding factor in this reverse had been the revolt of the Liberals and the Nonconformist members at the revelations of the life that was led at No.¬†10, Downing Street, during Sir James BrownвАЩs tenancy. The Evening Mail had a leading article, which drew a fine analogy between Public and Domestic Purity, between sobriety in the family and in the State.

There was another small paragraph which interested Adam.

Tragedy in West-End Hotel.

The death occurred early this morning at a private hotel in Dover Street of Miss Florence Ducane, described as being of independent means, following an accident in which Miss Ducane fell from a chandelier which she was attempting to mend. The inquest will be held tomorrow, which will be followed by the cremation at Golders Green. Miss Ducane, who was formerly connected with the stage, was well known in business circles.

Which only showed, thought Adam, how much better Lottie Crump knew the business of avoiding undesirable publicity than Sir James Brown.

When Adam reached London the rain had stopped, but there was a thin fog drifting in belts before a damp wind. The station was crowded with office workers hurrying with attach√© cases and evening papers to catch their evening trains home, coughing and sneezing as they went. They still wore their poppies. Adam went to a telephone-box and rang up Nina. She had left a message for him that she was having cocktails at Margot MetrolandвАЩs house. He drove to ShepheardвАЩs.

вАЬLottie,вАЭ he said, вАЬIвАЩve got a thousand pounds.вАЭ

вАЬHave you, now,вАЭ said Lottie indifferently. She lived on the assumption that everyone she knew always had several thousand pounds. It was to her as though he had said, вАЬLottie, I have a tall hat.вАЭ

вАЬCan you lend me some money till tomorrow till I cash the cheque?вАЭ

вАЬWhat a boy you are for borrowing. Just like your poor father. Here, you in the corner, lend Mr.¬†What-dвАЩyou-call-him some money.вАЭ

A tall Guardsman shook his retreating forehead and twirled his moustaches.

вАЬNo good coming to me, Lottie,вАЭ he said in a voice trained to command.

вАЬMean hound,вАЭ said Lottie. вАЬWhereвАЩs that American?вАЭ

Judge Skimp, who, since his experiences that morning, had become profoundly Anglophile, produced two tenpound notes. вАЬI shall be only too proud and honouredвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ he said.

вАЬGood old Judge Thingummy,вАЭ said Lottie. вАЬThatвАЩs the way.вАЭ

Adam hurried out into the hall as another bottle of champagne popped festively in the parlour.

вАЬDoge, ring up the Daimler Hire Company and order a car in my name. Tell it to go round to Lady MetrolandвАЩsвБ†вАФPastmaster House, Hill Street,вАЭ he said. Then he put on his hat and walked down Hay Hill, swinging an umbrella and laughing again, only more quietly, to himself.

At Lady MetrolandвАЩs he kept on his coat and waited in the hall.

вАЬWill you please tell Miss Blount IвАЩve called for her? No, I wonвАЩt go up.вАЭ

He looked at the hats on the table. Clearly there was quite a party. Two or three silk hats of people who had dressed early, the rest soft and black like his own. Then he began to dance again, jigging to himself in simple high spirits.

In a minute Nina came down the broad Adam staircase.

вАЬDarling, why didnвАЩt you come up? ItвАЩs so rude. Margot is longing to see you.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm so sorry, Nina. I couldnвАЩt face a party. IвАЩm so excited.вАЭ

вАЬWhy, whatвАЩs happened?вАЭ

вАЬEverything. IвАЩll tell you in the car.вАЭ

вАЬCar?вАЭ

вАЬYes, itвАЩll be here in a minute. WeвАЩre going down to the country for dinner. I canвАЩt tell you how clever IвАЩve been.вАЭ

вАЬBut what have you done, darling? Do stop dancing about.вАЭ

вАЬCanвАЩt stop. YouвАЩve no idea how clever I am.вАЭ

вАЬAdam. Are you tight again?вАЭ

вАЬLook out of the window and see if you can see a Daimler waiting.вАЭ

вАЬAdam, what have you been doing? I will be told.вАЭ

вАЬLook,вАЭ said Adam, producing the cheque. вАЬWhatcher think of that?вАЭ he added in Cockney.

вАЬMy dear, a thousand pounds. Did papa give you that?вАЭ

вАЬI earned it,вАЭ said Adam. вАЬOh, I earned it. You should have seen the luncheon I ate and the jokes I read. IвАЩm going to be married tomorrow. Oh, Nina, would Margot hate it if I sang in her hall?вАЭ

вАЬSheвАЩd simply loathe it, darling, and so should I. IвАЩm going to take care of that cheque. You remember what happened the last time you were given a thousand pounds.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs what your papa said.вАЭ

вАЬDid you tell him that?вАЭ

вАЬI told him everythingвБ†вАФand he gave me a thousand pounds.вАЭ

вАЬвА¶¬†Poor AdamвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ said Nina suddenly.

вАЬWhy did you say that?вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt know.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I believe this is your car.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬNina, why did you say вАШPoor AdamвАЩ?вАЭ

вАЬвА¶¬†Did I?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Oh, I donвАЩt know.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Oh, I do adore you so.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm going to be married tomorrow. Are you?вАЭ

вАЬYes, I expect so, dear.вАЭ

The chauffeur got rather bored while they tried to decide where they would dine. At every place he suggested they gave a little wail of dismay. вАЬBut thatвАЩs sure to be full of awful people we know,вАЭ they said. Maidenhead, Thame, Brighton, he suggested. Finally they decided to go to Arundel.

вАЬItвАЩll be nearly nine before we get there,вАЭ the chauffeur said. вАЬNow thereвАЩs a very nice hotel at Bray.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

But they went to Arundel.

вАЬWeвАЩll be married tomorrow,вАЭ said Adam in the car. вАЬAnd we wonвАЩt ask anybody to the wedding at all. And weвАЩll go abroad at once, and just not come back till IвАЩve written all those books. Nina, isnвАЩt it divine? Where shall we go?вАЭ

вАЬAnywhere you like, only rather warm, donвАЩt you think?вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt believe you really think we are going to be married, Nina, do you, or do you?вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt knowвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ itвАЩs only that I donвАЩt believe that really divine things like that ever do happen.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I donвАЩt know why.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Oh, I do like you so much tonight. If only you knew how sweet you looked skipping about in MargotвАЩs hall all by yourself. IвАЩd been watching you for hours before I came down.вАЭ

вАЬI shall send the car back,вАЭ said Adam, as they drove through Pulborough. вАЬWe can go home by train.вАЭ

вАЬIf there is a train.вАЭ

вАЬThereвАЩs bound to be,вАЭ said Adam. But this raised a question in both their minds that had been unobtrusively agitating them throughout the journey. Neither said any more on the subject, but there was a distinct air of constraint in the Daimler from Pulborough onwards.

This question was settled when they reached the hotel at Arundel.

вАЬWe want dinner,вАЭ said Adam, вАЬand a room for the night.вАЭ

вАЬDarling, am I going to be seduced?вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm afraid you are. Do you mind terribly?вАЭ

вАЬNot as much as all that,вАЭ said Nina, and added in Cockney, вАЬCharmed, IвАЩm sure.вАЭ

Everyone had finished dinner. They dined alone in a corner of the coffee-room, while the other waiters laid the tables for breakfast, looking at them resentfully. It was the dreariest kind of English dinner. After dinner the lounge was awful; there were some golfers in dinner-jackets playing bridge, and two old ladies. Adam and Nina went across the stable-yard to the taproom and sat until closing time in a warm haze of tobacco smoke listening to the intermittent gossip of the townspeople. They sat hand-in-hand, unembarrassed; after the first minute no one noticed them. Just before closing time Adam stood a round of drinks. They said:

вАЬGood health, sir. Best respects, madam,вАЭ and the barman said, вАЬCome along, please. Finish your drinks, please,вАЭ in a peculiar singsong tone.

There was a clock chiming as they crossed the yard and a slightly drunk farmer trying to start up his car. Then they went up an oak staircase lined with blunderbusses and coaching prints to their room.

They had no luggage (the chambermaid remarked on this next day to the young man who worked at the wireless shop, saying that that was the worst of being in a main road hotel. You got all sorts).

Adam undressed very quickly and got into bed; Nina more slowly arranging her clothes on the chair and fingering the ornaments on the chimneypiece with less than her usual self-possession. At last she put out the light.

вАЬDo you know,вАЭ she said, trembling slightly as she got into bed, вАЬthis is the first time this has happened to me?вАЭ

вАЬItвАЩs great fun,вАЭ said Adam, вАЬI promise you.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm sure it is,вАЭ said Nina seriously, вАЬI wasnвАЩt saying anything against it. I was only saying that it hadnвАЩt happened before.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Oh, Adam.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬAnd you said that really divine things didnвАЩt happen,вАЭ said Adam in the middle of the night.

вАЬI donвАЩt think that this is at all divine,вАЭ said Nina. вАЬItвАЩs given me a pain. AndвБ†вАФmy dear, that reminds me. IвАЩve something terribly important to say to you in the morning.вАЭ

вАЬWhat?вАЭ

вАЬNot now, darling. LetвАЩs go to sleep for a little, donвАЩt you think?вАЭ

Before Nina was properly awake Adam dressed and went out into the rain to get a shave. He came back bringing two toothbrushes and a bright red celluloid comb. Nina sat up in bed and combed her hair. She put AdamвАЩs coat over her back.

вАЬMy dear, you look exactly like La Vie Parisienne,вАЭ said Adam, turning round from brushing his teeth.

Then she threw off the coat and jumped out of bed, and he told her that she looked like a fashion drawing without the clothes. Nina was rather pleased about that, but she said that it was cold and that she still had a pain, only not so bad as it was. Then she dressed and they went downstairs.

Everyone else had had breakfast and the waiters were laying the tables for luncheon.

вАЬBy the way,вАЭ said Adam. вАЬYou said there was something you wanted to say.вАЭ

вАЬOh, yes, so there is. My dear, something quite awful.вАЭ

вАЬDo tell me.вАЭ

вАЬWell, itвАЩs about that cheque papa gave you. IвАЩm afraid it wonвАЩt help us as much as you thought.вАЭ

вАЬBut, darling, itвАЩs a thousand pounds, isnвАЩt it?вАЭ

вАЬJust look at it, my sweet.вАЭ She took it out of her bag and handed it across the table.

вАЬI donвАЩt see anything wrong with it,вАЭ said Adam.

вАЬNot the signature?вАЭ

вАЬWhy, good lord, the old idiotвАЩs signed it вАШCharlie Chaplin.вАЩвАКвАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs what I mean, darling.вАЭ

вАЬBut canвАЩt we get him to alter it? He must be dotty. IвАЩll go down and see him again today.вАЭ

вАЬI shouldnвАЩt do that, dearвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ donвАЩt you see.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Of course, heвАЩs very old, andвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I dare say you may have made things sound a little oddвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ donвАЩt you think, dear, he must have thought you a little dotty?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I meanвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ perhapsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ that cheque was a kind of joke.вАЭ

вАЬWell IвАЩm damnedвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ this really is a bore. When everything seemed to be going so well, too. When did you notice the signature, Nina?вАЭ

вАЬAs soon as you showed it to me, at MargotвАЩs. Only you looked so happy I didnвАЩt like to say anything.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ You did look happy, you know, Adam, and so sweet. I think I really fell in love with you for the first time when I saw you dancing all alone in the hall.вАЭ

вАЬWell IвАЩm damned,вАЭ said Adam again. вАЬThe old devil.вАЭ

вАЬAnyway, youвАЩve had some fun out of it, havenвАЩt youвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ or havenвАЩt you?вАЭ

вАЬHavenвАЩt you?вАЭ

вАЬMy dear, I never hated anything so much in my lifeвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ still, as long as you enjoyed it thatвАЩs something.вАЭ

вАЬI say, Nina,вАЭ said Adam after some time, вАЬwe shanвАЩt be able to get married after all.вАЭ

вАЬNo, IвАЩm afraid not.вАЭ

вАЬIt is a bore, isnвАЩt it?вАЭ

Later he said, вАЬI expect that parson thought I was dotty too.вАЭ

And later, вАЬAs a matter of fact, itвАЩs rather a good joke, donвАЩt you think?вАЭ

вАЬI think itвАЩs divine.вАЭ

In the train Nina said: вАЬItвАЩs awful to think that I shall probably never, as long as I live, see you dancing like that again all by yourself.вАЭ
